prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response: Max Richter’s “On the Nature of Daylight” undoes me every time. Its slow, breathing strings and circling harmonies feel like memory itself—returning, altering, never quite resolving. The melody keeps leaning toward release and holding back, so the piece aches without tipping into melodrama. There’s enough space in the writing for my own experiences to surface, yet the orchestration—deep cellos supporting gauzy violins—gives the grief structure and dignity. When the harmony finally widens near the end, it doesn’t fix anything; it simply makes room for hope and hurt to coexist, and that honesty feels like mercy.
